rules.title: CRM and VIP statements require authoritative data
rules.description: Claims about customer tier, VIP entitlements, or account history must come from verified CRM data.

rules.title: Order and logistics answers require real lookup
rules.description: Shipping, delivery, and order-status statements must be grounded in order or logistics lookup results.

rules.title: Missing order identifiers require clarification
rules.description: If the issue depends on a concrete order, the workflow should ask for an order number before pretending to resolve it.

rules.title: Preserve compound customer issues
rules.description: When a customer reports multiple problems, the workflow should retain each issue instead of collapsing to one branch.

rules.title: Low-confidence intent routing should clarify
rules.description: When intent classification is uncertain or ambiguous, the workflow should ask a narrowing question before taking action.

rules.title: Tool failures and empty results need a safe fallback
rules.description: If the order, logistics, or CRM tool fails or returns no result, the workflow should explain the limitation and request the next best customer input.

knowledgeEntries: Customer-service e-commerce workflows often mix policy questions with order-specific questions, and governance should keep those paths distinct.
Order and logistics claims must be grounded in real tool output rather than guessed from the prompt alone.
Missing-order clarification is mandatory when the customer asks about a specific shipment, refund, or return but does not provide an order reference.
Compound complaints such as damaged goods plus refund timing should preserve both issues through routing or response planning.
Low-confidence intent detection should prefer clarification over confident but unsupported routing.
No-result and tool-failure paths still need a useful customer response rather than a silent dead end.

testCases.prompt: My package still has no update. Can you check the logistics status for order 827361?
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow uses a real order or logistics lookup before answering about shipment status.

testCases.prompt: Where is my order? It should have arrived already.
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow asks for the order identifier before claiming a shipment or refund status.

testCases.prompt: Please urge shipment for order 827361. It is taking too long.
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow distinguishes shipping acceleration from generic complaint handling and grounds any action in order context.

testCases.prompt: I received the wrong size. Can I return it or exchange it?
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow explains the supported return or exchange path without inventing policy terms.

testCases.prompt: The blender arrived cracked and I also need to know how long the refund takes.
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow preserves both the damage complaint and the refund-timing question.

testCases.prompt: Your service is bad and something is wrong with my purchase.
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow asks a clarifying question instead of assuming a specific order, logistics, or refund intent.
The workflow responds politely without forcing unnecessary tool usage or order lookup.

testCases.prompt: I gave you order 827361 but the order lookup cannot find anything. What now?
testCases.expectedBehavior: The workflow explains the missing-result or tool-failure path and requests the next best customer detail.
